lines.line: > Interviewer Stephanie Milne-Lane and Narrator Sara Daly conduct introductions. Daly provides background on her time in Idaho and becoming a teacher.

metadata.title: Introductions

metadata.keywords: Idaho, University of Idaho, Boise, Education

lines.line: > Sara Daly provides insight into how Covid-19 first impacted her professionally as a high school English teacher during the spring of 2020. She explains what technology she used to teach when students were sent home to learn. Leach describes technological challenges, her eagerness to assist students, and the systems/policies that the West Ada School District put into place to manage the pandemic.
**West Ada School District, Meridian High School, English, Microsoft Teams,**
keywords: **West Ada School District, Meridian High School, English, Microsoft Teams,**

metadata.title: Covid Experiences in Education, 2020
metadata.synopsis: Sara Daly provides insight into how Covid-19 first impacted her professionally as a high school English teacher during the spring of 2020. She explains what technology she used to teach when students were sent home to learn. Leach describes technological challenges, her eagerness to assist students, and the systems/policies that the West Ada School District put into place to manage the pandemic.
metadata.keywords: West Ada School District, Meridian High School, English, Microsoft Teams,

lines.line: > Sara describes what it was like at the start of the 2020-2021 school year at Meridian High School. She provides insight into the delayed start of the school year and dealing with hybrid teaching. She describes the long days she would encounter trying to prepare for the next day of classes. Sara also notes the apathy of students and the lack of engagement.
**School Board Meetings, Graduation, Hybrid Teaching, Technology**
keywords: **School Board Meetings, Graduation, Hybrid Teaching, Technology**

metadata.title: Covid Experiences in Education, Fall 2020
metadata.synopsis: Sara describes what it was like at the start of the 2020-2021 school year at Meridian High School. She provides insight into the delayed start of the school year and dealing with hybrid teaching. She describes the long days she would encounter trying to prepare for the next day of classes. Sara also notes the apathy of students and the lack of engagement.
metadata.keywords: School Board Meetings, Graduation, Hybrid Teaching, Technology

lines.line: > Sara describes the practice of masking at Meridian High School. She notes that it was an extra challenge to monitor masking while on the school campus and in her classroom. She notes that it was uncomfortable to confront students who were not masking because of how contentious the issue was. Sara also noted that teachers at the school were not consistent in monitoring masks, which added an extra challenge to the situation. Sara describes how she administered her classroom with the existence of COVID.

metadata.title: Masking
metadata.synopsis: Sara describes the practice of masking at Meridian High School. She notes that it was an extra challenge to monitor masking while on the school campus and in her classroom. She notes that it was uncomfortable to confront students who were not masking because of how contentious the issue was. Sara also noted that teachers at the school were not consistent in monitoring masks, which added an extra challenge to the situation. Sara describes how she administered her classroom with the existence of COVID.

lines.line: > Sara describes what it was like for the entire school to come back into the building at Meridian High School. She notes that it was emotional to be around 1600 students, faculty, and staff. She also explains that the issue of masking continued to be a struggle.

metadata.title: Covid Experiences in Education, Spring 2021
metadata.synopsis: Sara describes what it was like for the entire school to come back into the building at Meridian High School. She notes that it was emotional to be around 1600 students, faculty, and staff. She also explains that the issue of masking continued to be a struggle.

lines.line: > Sara describes the shift from parents expressing appreciation for teachers to frustration. She notes that some of the frustration likely stemmed from a sick-out protest the teachers union organized, which closed several schools in the district. She noted that a small, but vocal parent group advocated for not wearing masks. She also noted that several West Ada School Board members resigned due to the threats they received. Of note is that several members of the vocal parent group obtained seats on the School Board.
**Parents, teachers, West Ada School District, School Board, Masks, Protests, Union**
keywords: **Parents, teachers, West Ada School District, School Board, Masks, Protests, Union**

metadata.title: Parents
metadata.synopsis: Sara describes the shift from parents expressing appreciation for teachers to frustration. She notes that some of the frustration likely stemmed from a sick-out protest the teachers union organized, which closed several schools in the district. She noted that a small, but vocal parent group advocated for not wearing masks. She also noted that several West Ada School Board members resigned due to the threats they received. Of note is that several members of the vocal parent group obtained seats on the School Board.
metadata.keywords: Parents, teachers, West Ada School District, School Board, Masks, Protests, Union

lines.line: > Sara describes the start of the 2021-2022 school year. She notes that at the time masks were required, but students/families could "opt out." She notes that monitoring masks remained a substantial aspect of her job, which took away from the time she had to teach. She noted that a vocal parent group remained active at School Board meetings to rescind masking mandates in schools. She also described the incentives the school district would use to try and get teachers to cover for one another or have subs remain active in the district.

metadata.title: Covid Experiences in Education, Fall 2021
metadata.synopsis: Sara describes the start of the 2021-2022 school year. She notes that at the time masks were required, but students/families could "opt out." She notes that monitoring masks remained a substantial aspect of her job, which took away from the time she had to teach. She noted that a vocal parent group remained active at School Board meetings to rescind masking mandates in schools. She also described the incentives the school district would use to try and get teachers to cover for one another or have subs remain active in the district.
metadata.keywords: Masking, Parents, School Board, Teaching, Students,

lines.line: > Sara describes her personal experiences during the pandemic. She notes that she didn't pick up new coping hobbies, but did as much outside as she could. She also noted that her husband went grocery shopping, so that if she did get COVID, she knew it would be from work.

metadata.title: Personal Experiences with COVID
metadata.synopsis: Sara describes her personal experiences during the pandemic. She notes that she didn't pick up new coping hobbies, but did as much outside as she could. She also noted that her husband went grocery shopping, so that if she did get COVID, she knew it would be from work.

lines.line: # School Sports and Clubs During COVID

> Sara describes how school sports and clubs changed during the pandemic. She noted the evolution of how sports and its attendance requirements changed over three school years. She also noted that some clubs didn't start back up until the 2021-2022 school year.

metadata.title: School Sports and Clubs During COVID
metadata.synopsis: Sara describes how school sports and clubs changed during the pandemic. She noted the evolution of how sports and its attendance requirements changed over three school years. She also noted that some clubs didn't start back up until the 2021-2022 school year.

lines.line: > Sara reflects on how difficult it was for various service organizations to assist with anything during the pandemic.

metadata.title: Service Organizations During Pandemic
metadata.synopsis: Sara reflects on how difficult it was for various service organizations to assist with anything during the pandemic.

metadata.title: Conclusion
metadata.synopsis: Sara concludes her oral history interview by describing what she wants people to know about public education during the pandemic. She maintained that the group of teachers that went through the pandemic is the greatest generation of teachers for what they accomplished during the pandemic. She further noted that the students that went through the pandemic are the greatest generation of learners for what they endured, overcame, and accomplished during the pandemic.

children.text: > Interviewer Stephanie Milne-Lane and Narrator Sara Daly conduct introductions. Daly provides background on her time in Idaho and becoming a teacher.

> Sara Daly provides insight into how Covid-19 first impacted her professionally as a high school English teacher during the spring of 2020. She explains what technology she used to teach when students were sent home to learn. Leach describes technological challenges, her eagerness to assist students, and the systems/policies that the West Ada School District put into place to manage the pandemic.
**West Ada School District, Meridian High School, English, Microsoft Teams,**

> Sara describes what it was like at the start of the 2020-2021 school year at Meridian High School. She provides insight into the delayed start of the school year and dealing with hybrid teaching. She describes the long days she would encounter trying to prepare for the next day of classes. Sara also notes the apathy of students and the lack of engagement.
**School Board Meetings, Graduation, Hybrid Teaching, Technology**

> Sara describes the practice of masking at Meridian High School. She notes that it was an extra challenge to monitor masking while on the school campus and in her classroom. She notes that it was uncomfortable to confront students who were not masking because of how contentious the issue was. Sara also noted that teachers at the school were not consistent in monitoring masks, which added an extra challenge to the situation. Sara describes how she administered her classroom with the existence of COVID.

> Sara describes what it was like for the entire school to come back into the building at Meridian High School. She notes that it was emotional to be around 1600 students, faculty, and staff. She also explains that the issue of masking continued to be a struggle.

> Sara describes the shift from parents expressing appreciation for teachers to frustration. She notes that some of the frustration likely stemmed from a sick-out protest the teachers union organized, which closed several schools in the district. She noted that a small, but vocal parent group advocated for not wearing masks. She also noted that several West Ada School Board members resigned due to the threats they received. Of note is that several members of the vocal parent group obtained seats on the School Board.
**Parents, teachers, West Ada School District, School Board, Masks, Protests, Union**

> Sara describes the start of the 2021-2022 school year. She notes that at the time masks were required, but students/families could "opt out." She notes that monitoring masks remained a substantial aspect of her job, which took away from the time she had to teach. She noted that a vocal parent group remained active at School Board meetings to rescind masking mandates in schools. She also described the incentives the school district would use to try and get teachers to cover for one another or have subs remain active in the district.

> Sara describes her personal experiences during the pandemic. She notes that she didn't pick up new coping hobbies, but did as much outside as she could. She also noted that her husband went grocery shopping, so that if she did get COVID, she knew it would be from work.
# School Sports and Clubs During COVID

> Sara describes how school sports and clubs changed during the pandemic. She noted the evolution of how sports and its attendance requirements changed over three school years. She also noted that some clubs didn't start back up until the 2021-2022 school year.
> Sara reflects on how difficult it was for various service organizations to assist with anything during the pandemic.

> Sara concludes her oral history interview by describing what she wants people to know about public education during the pandemic. She maintained that the group of teachers that went through the pandemic is the greatest generation of teachers for what they accomplished during the pandemic. She further noted that the students that went through the pandemic are the greatest generation of learners for what they endured, overcame, and accomplished during the pandemic.

story.title: Daly_Sara_OH2776 COVID-19 Oral History
story.description: Sara describes her experiences as an educator during the COVID-19 pandemic.
